title: Sample Claude Exam - Form C
description: Generated 60-question variation bank aligned to the Claude architecture foundations study guide.

source.notes: Questions were generated by domain-focused workers, then validated and enriched against the structured study guide.

domains.label: Agentic Architecture & Orchestration
domains.description: Design model-driven agent loops, coordinator-subagent systems, handoffs, hooks, decomposition, and session state.

domains.label: Tool Design & MCP Integration
domains.description: Design reliable tools, MCP integrations, structured errors, scoped tool access, and built-in tool usage.

domains.label: Claude Code Configuration & Workflows
domains.description: Configure Claude Code memory, rules, skills, commands, plan mode, iterative workflows, and CI behavior.

domains.label: Prompt Engineering & Structured Output
domains.description: Improve precision, use few-shot prompting, enforce schema output, validate retries, batch work, and design review architectures.

domains.label: Context Management & Reliability
domains.description: Preserve facts, manage long context, escalate appropriately, propagate errors, explore codebases, calibrate review, and keep provenance.

guideReferences: Domain 1, Task 1.1: Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.guideSummary: Agentic loop completion should be based on stop_reason, with tool_use causing another iteration and end_turn ending the loop.
questions.prompt: A benefits assistant sometimes outputs, "I can answer now," and also includes a `tool_use` request for `get_plan_limits` in the same model response. The application treats the phrase as final and skips the tool. What should the loop controller do?

choices.text: Ignore tool calls whenever the text indicates the answer is already known.
choices.explanation: This preserves the bug by trusting prose over the model's formal tool-use signal.

choices.text: Honor the `tool_use` stop_reason, execute `get_plan_limits`, append the result, and ask the model for the next step.
choices.explanation: Correct. Tool use is the control signal; natural-language confidence should not terminate the loop.

choices.text: Ask the user whether the assistant should call `get_plan_limits` before continuing.
choices.explanation: The agentic loop is designed to execute model-selected tools without unnecessary user arbitration.

choices.text: Disable `get_plan_limits` for questions where the model sounds confident.
choices.explanation: Confidence wording is not a reliable tool-selection or completion mechanism.

questions.guideSummary: Overloaded tools with free-text modes should be split or constrained when distinct tasks need distinct input and output contracts.
questions.prompt: A finance document agent exposes one MCP tool, `process_document(documentId, instruction)`. Users ask it to extract invoice totals, compare contract terms, and summarize audit notes. Evaluation shows totals are sometimes returned as prose and summaries sometimes include invented numeric fields. What interface change best addresses the reliability issue?

choices.text: Split the tool into purpose-specific tools such as `extract_invoice_totals`, `compare_contract_terms`, and `summarize_audit_notes`, each with its own schema and boundaries.
choices.explanation: Correct. Distinct contracts reduce ambiguity and let Claude select the right capability and output shape.

choices.text: Keep the single tool and ask Claude to write more detailed instructions in the `instruction` field.
choices.explanation: The problem is the free-text contract itself; more prose does not enforce distinct output semantics.

choices.text: Add a `temperature` parameter to the tool so extraction calls can run at a lower value.
choices.explanation: Tool interface boundaries, not sampling settings, are the primary issue described by the guide.

choices.text: Route every document through the audit-summary path first, then post-process for totals or terms.
choices.explanation: Summarization can lose exact financial values and does not replace task-specific extraction or comparison.

questions.guideSummary: Covers diagnosing team inconsistency caused by instructions living in user memory instead of project memory.
questions.prompt: Two engineers ask Claude Code to add API endpoints in the same repo. One gets generated handlers that include the team's required audit logging; the other does not. Investigation shows the audit rule exists only in the first engineer's ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. What should the team do?

choices.text: Tell the second engineer to add the first engineer's home directory as an import source.
choices.explanation: A teammate's home directory is not a durable or reviewable source for team configuration.

choices.text: Create a user-level slash command that adds audit logging after endpoints are generated.
choices.explanation: This makes a required standard optional and personal instead of consistently loaded for the project.

choices.text: Move the audit logging requirement to project-level CLAUDE.md or an imported project rule file.
choices.explanation: Correct. Shared coding requirements belong in repository-controlled memory so every teammate loads the same standard.

choices.text: Ask Claude Code to inspect git history for audit logging examples instead of using memory.
choices.explanation: Examples can help, but they do not replace explicit shared instructions for a required convention.

guideReferences: Domain 4, Task 4.1: Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
questions.guideSummary: Covers defining severity with concrete examples so review findings are actionable and trusted.
questions.prompt: A code-review bot labels every unhandled exception path as 'critical.' Developers push back because many are test-only utilities or commands where the platform already retries safely. Real production paths without user-facing recovery should still be caught. Which prompt change is best?

choices.text: Tell the bot to avoid using the word 'critical' unless the code has a security vulnerability.
choices.explanation: This changes wording but does not define how to classify reliability issues.

choices.text: Ask the bot to report only one exception-handling issue per review.
choices.explanation: A limit hides possible real issues and still may select a false positive.

choices.text: Define severity levels with concrete examples, including skip rules for test-only utilities and report rules for production user-impacting paths.
choices.explanation: Correct. Severity calibration needs explicit examples and category boundaries, not a blanket critical label.

choices.text: Remove exception handling from the review scope because it produced too many false positives.
choices.explanation: That throws away a legitimate risk category instead of calibrating it.

skills: Maintain structured case facts.
Persist multi-issue state separately from summaries.
Trim tool results to relevant fields.
Place summaries early and use explicit section headers.
knowledge: Progressive summaries can blur amounts, dates, and commitments.
Lost-in-the-middle effects hide middle input details.
Verbose tool results can consume context without adding value.
Complete conversation history helps coherence.
guideReferences: Domain 5, Task 5.1: Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions
questions.guideSummary: Persist multi-issue state separately from summaries so exact patient-provided facts remain available.
questions.prompt: A patient intake assistant collects medication allergies, a preferred pharmacy, insurance details, and a symptom timeline across a long chat. After a compaction, it remembers the symptoms but loses the penicillin allergy and asks for the pharmacy again. What should the implementation change?

choices.text: Disable compaction so all prior messages remain visible for every request.
choices.explanation: Long raw context can still suffer from lost-in-the-middle effects and may become impractical.

choices.text: Store a structured intake record for critical fields and open questions, update it after each turn, and include only relevant fields in subsequent prompts.
choices.explanation: Correct. Structured state preserves safety-critical facts and avoids depending on lossy summaries.

choices.text: Add a prompt instruction that allergies are important and should never be forgotten.
choices.explanation: A reminder helps prioritization but does not create durable state for facts that leave usable context.

choices.text: Ask the patient to repeat allergies, pharmacy, and insurance at the end of every intake session.
choices.explanation: Confirmation may be useful at final review, but repeated recovery questions are not a robust context strategy.

questions.guideSummary: Arbitrary iteration caps can be safety guards, but the main loop should continue on tool_use and terminate on end_turn.
questions.prompt: A travel rebooking agent is limited to exactly three tool iterations. Multi-city disruptions often require a fourth call to confirm hotel availability, so the agent stops with an incomplete itinerary. Which loop design is best?

choices.text: Increase the fixed limit from three calls to six calls for all rebooking sessions.
choices.explanation: A larger arbitrary cap still fails cases needing more calls and wastes work on simpler cases.

choices.text: Drive continuation from `tool_use` and completion from `end_turn`, while keeping a separate high-limit safety guard for runaway loops.
choices.explanation: Correct. The model's stop_reason should control normal loop progress; caps should not be the main completion test.

choices.text: Require the model to plan the exact number of tool calls before starting.
choices.explanation: The needed calls depend on intermediate tool results, so a fixed upfront count is brittle.

choices.text: Stop after the first successful tool result and ask the user to request follow-up actions.
choices.explanation: A successful result may be only one step in the task, not a completed itinerary.

questions.guideSummary: Tool descriptions are selection signals and should include when not to use the tool, expected inputs, and examples for edge cases.
questions.prompt: A security triage assistant has `search_logs` and `open_incident`. Because `open_incident` is described only as "opens incident data," Claude sometimes calls it to search raw logs for suspicious IP addresses. The incident tool is expensive and returns only already-created incidents. What is the best correction?

choices.text: Make `open_incident` cheaper so accidental calls are less costly.
choices.explanation: Lowering cost only reduces the price of the wrong call; the boundary confusion that makes Claude pick it for log search remains, so the misrouting continues.

choices.text: Improve `open_incident` to say it retrieves an existing incident by incident ID, is not for raw log search, and returns status, owner, timeline, and linked alerts.
choices.explanation: Correct. The description should clarify inputs, outputs, examples, and boundaries so Claude does not use it as a log-search substitute.

choices.text: Remove `search_logs` so `open_incident` becomes the only security tool.
choices.explanation: That removes the tool actually needed for raw log search, so Claude has no correct option left and the boundary problem gets worse, not better.

choices.text: Leave the description as is and add a system-prompt rule that suspicious-IP log searches must use `search_logs`, not `open_incident`.
choices.explanation: A prompt rule patches one phrasing but leaves the thin description Claude actually selects from unchanged, so other log-search requests still misroute; fixing the description addresses the root signal.

questions.guideSummary: Covers using /memory and modular imports to understand and correct loaded Claude Code instructions.
questions.prompt: Claude Code keeps applying an obsolete packaging rule that the team believes was removed. The repo has a root CLAUDE.md that imports several standards files, plus directory-level memory in two packages. What is the most reliable first diagnostic step?

choices.text: Delete all directory-level CLAUDE.md files and keep only the root file.
choices.explanation: That is a broad destructive change before confirming where the obsolete rule is loaded from.

choices.text: Move every imported standards file into user-level memory and retry the task.
choices.explanation: Project standards should remain shared through the repo, not moved to personal memory.

choices.text: Convert the packaging rule into a CI prompt so Claude Code no longer sees it locally.
choices.explanation: This does not diagnose the source of the stale local instruction and may create different behavior between local and CI runs.

choices.text: Use /memory to inspect which memory files and imports are currently loaded for the task.
choices.explanation: Correct. /memory helps diagnose hierarchy and import issues by showing the active memory context.

questions.guideSummary: Covers report-versus-skip criteria for prompt reviews where false positives reduce trust.
questions.prompt: A prompt audit assistant reviews agent instructions and reports every sentence that could be more specific. The team only wants findings that can cause unsafe tool use, wrong output format, or missed escalation. What is the best improvement?

choices.text: Keep the prompt broad but ask for a maximum of three audit findings.
choices.explanation: A maximum count does not distinguish harmful ambiguity from harmless phrasing.

choices.text: Require every finding to include a rewritten sentence.
choices.explanation: This makes comments more concrete, but it does not stop the assistant from reporting non-issues.

choices.text: Rewrite the audit prompt with report categories tied to those failure modes and skip examples for harmless wording preferences.
choices.explanation: Correct. Explicit criteria align findings with the team's actionable risks and reduce style-noise false positives.

choices.text: Use a JSON schema for findings so all reports have the same fields.
choices.explanation: Structure improves consistency, but the model still needs criteria for what belongs in the report.

skills: Add escalation criteria with examples.
Honor explicit human-agent requests immediately.
Acknowledge frustration while resolving straightforward cases.
Ask for identifiers rather than guessing among multiple matches.
knowledge: Escalation triggers include human requests, policy gaps, and inability to progress.
Straightforward issues may be resolved even when a customer is frustrated unless they request a human.
Sentiment and self-reported confidence are weak proxies for complexity.
Multiple matches require clarification.
guideReferences: Domain 5, Task 5.2: Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
questions.guideSummary: Resolve ambiguity by requesting identifiers rather than guessing among multiple plausible matches.
questions.prompt: A customer asks for the status of order 1048. The lookup tool returns three matching orders under different household members with the same last name and address. The customer is impatient but has not asked for a human. What should the agent do?

choices.text: Select the most recent matching order and answer quickly to reduce friction.
choices.explanation: Guessing among multiple matches can disclose or act on the wrong account.

choices.text: Ask for a clarifying identifier such as the email address, recipient name, or last four digits of the payment method.
choices.explanation: Correct. Multiple matches require clarification rather than guessing or sentiment-based escalation.

choices.text: Escalate immediately because multiple matches mean the agent cannot proceed.
choices.explanation: The agent can often recover by asking for a clarifying identifier before escalation.

choices.text: Provide status for all three orders but omit sensitive details.
choices.explanation: Even limited status across multiple household members can create a privacy and accuracy issue.

guideReferences: Domain 1, Task 1.2: Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns
questions.guideSummary: Coordinator-subagent systems should route specialist work through the coordinator for visibility, aggregation, and recovery.
questions.prompt: A customer operations system lets billing, entitlement, and technical-support agents hand cases directly to each other. When a case stalls, no component has the full state, and duplicate customer messages are sent. What orchestration pattern should replace this?

choices.text: Let the first specialist that receives a case own all later routing decisions.
choices.explanation: That merely moves the hidden state problem to whichever specialist starts first.

choices.text: Have all specialists write free-form notes into a shared document and read it before every reply.
choices.explanation: A shared note does not provide clear routing, aggregation, or recovery responsibilities.

choices.text: Use a coordinator as the hub that delegates to specialists, receives their results, tracks case state, and decides the next handoff.
choices.explanation: Correct. Hub-and-spoke orchestration preserves observability and recovery through the coordinator.

choices.text: Broadcast every customer message to all specialists and use the fastest response.
choices.explanation: Broadcasting increases duplicated work and can produce inconsistent answers.

Recover locally from transient failures where possible.

guideReferences: Domain 2, Task 2.2: Implement structured error responses for MCP tools
questions.guideSummary: Retry metadata should be explicit for transient MCP failures so agents do not guess when to retry or escalate.
questions.prompt: A ticketing MCP tool `create_ticket` sometimes receives a vendor 429 rate-limit response with a `retryAfterSeconds` value. Currently it returns `isError: true` with text "Request failed." Claude often retries immediately several times and then escalates. What should the tool return?

choices.text: `isError: false` with a placeholder ticket ID and a note that creation is pending.
choices.explanation: A fake success creates downstream state problems and hides the real failure.

choices.text: `isError: true` with the vendor's raw JSON copied into a text block only.
choices.explanation: Raw JSON may include the retry hint, but without normalized metadata Claude still has to infer policy.

choices.text: No MCP response; the server should sleep until the retry window passes and then block until success.
choices.explanation: Long blocking hides state from the agent and can tie up the workflow; the response should communicate recovery metadata.

choices.text: `isError: true` with `errorCategory: transient`, `isRetryable: true`, `retryAfterSeconds`, and a short explanation.
choices.explanation: Correct. The model needs explicit retryability and timing metadata to recover without wasteful immediate retries.

questions.guideSummary: Covers selecting project slash commands for shared, explicit workflows that should not always load.
questions.prompt: A data team has a repeatable local workflow for planning customer data migrations: gather affected tables, draft a rollback plan, and produce an approval checklist. It should be available to all repo contributors, but it should not consume context during unrelated coding tasks. What is the best fit?

choices.text: Keep the workflow steps in a team wiki page and have engineers paste them into the Claude Code prompt at the start of each migration.
choices.explanation: Manual copy-paste drifts from the wiki, is easy to do incompletely, and is not version-controlled with the repo. A project command keeps the steps maintained in one place and invoked reliably.

choices.text: Create a personal skill in one data engineer's home directory and ask others to copy it.
choices.explanation: Copying personal configuration is not a reliable way to share a team workflow.

choices.text: Use a path-specific rule that triggers on all SQL files.
choices.explanation: The workflow is not just a SQL file convention. It is an explicit planning process with multiple steps.

choices.text: Create a project slash command in .claude/commands/ for the migration planning workflow.
choices.explanation: Correct. A repository command is shared, version-controlled, and invoked only when needed.

guideReferences: Domain 4, Task 4.2: Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
questions.guideSummary: Covers using few-shot examples to reduce hallucination and improve consistent extraction.
questions.prompt: A resume parser sometimes infers skills from job titles, such as adding 'Kubernetes' for a platform engineer even when the resume never mentions Kubernetes. It also misses skills listed in project descriptions. Which prompt improvement best addresses both errors?

choices.text: Add few-shot examples showing explicitly mentioned skills in summaries, projects, and job bullets, plus counterexamples where inferred skills are omitted.
choices.explanation: Correct. Examples teach both recall across formats and the no-inference rule.

choices.text: Make the `skills` array required with a minimum length so every resume returns enough skills.
choices.explanation: Minimum lengths encourage invented entries when the source has fewer explicit skills.

choices.text: Ask the model to be creative when identifying likely skills from experience.
choices.explanation: That reinforces the hallucination pattern the team wants to remove.

choices.text: Run a second extraction pass that adds commonly associated skills based on job title.
choices.explanation: That intentionally adds inferred skills rather than source-grounded extraction.

skills: Return failure type, attempted query, partial results, and alternatives.
Distinguish empty successful results from failures.
Recover locally before escalating.
Annotate synthesis coverage gaps.
knowledge: Structured error context enables recovery decisions.
Access failures differ from valid empty results.
Generic errors hide useful recovery context.
Suppressing errors and terminating on single failures are both anti-patterns.
guideReferences: Domain 5, Task 5.3: Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
questions.guideSummary: Distinguish successful empty results from access failures so coordinators can synthesize accurately.
questions.prompt: A due-diligence coordinator asks two subagents to check sanctions exposure. The corporate-registry subagent finds no matches. The watchlist subagent cannot query two jurisdictions because its credential lacks access. How should the coordinator represent the result?

choices.text: Report no matches in the registry source and a separate coverage gap for the inaccessible watchlist jurisdictions, including attempted queries and recovery options.
choices.explanation: Correct. The synthesis should preserve the difference between empty results and failures.

choices.text: Report no sanctions exposure because the completed registry search had no matches.
choices.explanation: A valid empty result in one source does not cover an access failure in another source.

choices.text: Report that sanctions review failed completely because not every source was checked.
choices.explanation: This overstates the failure and discards useful completed-source evidence.

choices.text: Retry the watchlist subagent until access succeeds, then provide a final answer only after all sources complete.
choices.explanation: A credential access failure is not usually solved by blind retrying, and the coordinator still needs structured failure context.

questions.guideSummary: After specialists return, the coordinator must aggregate and reconcile their outputs -- resolving conflicts and filling detected gaps -- rather than concatenating reports.
questions.prompt: A market research coordinator delegates to web, financial, and regulatory specialists, each of which returns a correctly scoped report. The coordinator then concatenates the three reports into the final brief verbatim. Reviewers find that the financial specialist's revenue forecast assumes a launch date the regulatory specialist flagged as infeasible, and neither report addresses pricing because each assumed the other would. The contradiction and the gap reach the customer unresolved. What should the coordinator do?

choices.text: Tell each specialist to read the other specialists' reports and resolve any conflicts among themselves before returning.
choices.explanation: That pushes reconciliation onto siblings, which lacks a single owner and can produce conflicting fixes; the coordinator should own aggregation.

choices.text: Run an aggregation step that reconciles cross-specialist conflicts, surfaces the unaddressed pricing gap, and requests targeted follow-up before producing the brief.
choices.explanation: Correct. Aggregation and gap handling across returned specialist outputs are coordinator responsibilities, not something concatenation provides.

choices.text: Add a final instruction asking the model to make the concatenated brief read smoothly.
choices.explanation: Smoothing the prose hides the conflicting launch-date assumption and still leaves the pricing gap unfilled.

choices.text: Give the regulatory specialist the last word and drop any financial findings that disagree with it.
choices.explanation: Discarding conflicting findings instead of reconciling them loses valid analysis and still does not address the missing pricing coverage.

questions.guideSummary: Business-rule errors should be non-retryable and include safe explanations the agent can use with the customer.
questions.prompt: A `cancel_subscription` MCP tool rejects cancellations for accounts under legal hold. The current error text is "operation denied," so Claude asks the user to retry later or provide a different account email. What response is most appropriate?

choices.text: Return `isError: true`, `errorCategory: business_rule`, `isRetryable: false`, and a customer-safe explanation that cancellation is unavailable due to account restrictions.
choices.explanation: Correct. The agent should know this is not a transient failure and should have language it can safely present.

choices.text: Return `isError: false` with `cancelled: false` so the agent does not treat the response as a failure.
choices.explanation: A denied cancellation is a failure of the requested operation and needs explicit error semantics.

choices.text: Return `isRetryable: true` because legal hold status might change in the future.
choices.explanation: Retryability should describe the current workflow action, not a possible future policy change.

choices.text: Return only the internal legal-hold reason code so Claude can decide how much to reveal.
choices.explanation: The tool should provide a readable, customer-safe explanation rather than exposing raw internal codes alone.

questions.guideSummary: Covers keeping personal skill variants out of shared project configuration while preserving team workflows.
questions.prompt: The repo has a shared bug-triage skill. One engineer wants a personal variant that adds extra exploratory logging checks and uses their preferred note format. The team does not want that variant to affect everyone else. What should the engineer do?

choices.text: Edit the shared skill so every teammate gets the extra logging checks by default.
choices.explanation: This changes the team workflow for a personal preference and may add noise for others.

choices.text: Create the variant as a personal skill outside the repository-scoped shared configuration.
choices.explanation: Correct. Personal skill variants should avoid changing teammates' behavior.

choices.text: Add the preferred note format to root CLAUDE.md so all future tasks use it.
choices.explanation: A personal output preference should not become an always-loaded project instruction.

choices.text: Create a path-scoped rule for files the engineer usually edits.
choices.explanation: Path rules are for project conventions by file path, not personal workflow variants.

questions.prompt: A support triage prompt asks Claude to output `billing`, `technical`, or `policy` for each ticket. Tickets mentioning both a failed payment and a feature outage are classified inconsistently. The downstream router needs one primary category plus a rationale. What should you add?

choices.text: A rule to always choose the first category mentioned in the customer's message.
choices.explanation: First-mentioned intent is arbitrary and can route urgent technical outages incorrectly.

choices.text: A larger enum containing every possible pair of categories.
choices.explanation: Expanding categories complicates routing and does not teach how to select a primary category.

choices.text: A request for Claude to include all possible categories whenever a ticket is ambiguous.
choices.explanation: The router needs one primary category, not an unresolved set.

choices.text: Few-shot examples for mixed-intent tickets that show the desired primary category and short rationale.

choices.text: Glob fits: the criterion is the filename pattern itself, which is exactly the path-matching job Glob does, returning the full set of test paths.
choices.explanation: Correct. The selection criterion is the file name, not anything inside the files, so Glob's path matching enumerates the test files directly without reading their contents.

choices.text: Grep is fine here because test files are source files, and matching `.spec.ts` or `.test.ts` is just a pattern match Grep performs.
choices.explanation: Grep matches against file contents, not file names, so it would search inside files for that literal text instead of enumerating files by their name pattern.

choices.text: Use Bash with `find` and a name filter, since shell globbing is more reliable than the built-in for this kind of enumeration.
choices.explanation: A Bash find can list the files but re-implements what Glob already does cleanly, adding shell-quoting and portability risk where the built-in path tool is the direct fit.

choices.text: Use Grep first to find every file that imports the test runner, treating those import lines as the way to identify test files.
choices.explanation: Runner imports are a noisy proxy and can miss or over-match files; the task defines tests by name pattern, which path matching answers exactly.

choices.text: Run a fixed pipeline that fully checks schema, timezones, currency, duplication, and source freshness in that order on every reconciliation.

choices.text: Map the candidate causes, inspect the largest discrepancy signal first, and adapt the next diagnostic subtasks based on what each result reveals.
choices.explanation: Correct. Open-ended investigations call for adaptive decomposition driven by intermediate findings rather than a fixed sequence.

choices.text: Assume the most common historical cause, a timezone bug, and investigate only that before reporting.
choices.explanation: Committing to a single hypothesis before gathering evidence risks missing the actual cause.

questions.prompt: A vendor-risk review session is three weeks old. Since then, the vendor replaced its security whitepaper, changed subprocessors, and updated its incident history. The team wants a current recommendation. What session strategy is best?

choices.text: Resume the old session because it contains the most complete prior analysis.
choices.explanation: Completeness is not enough when the underlying sources changed materially.

choices.text: Fork the old session and let each branch update one changed artifact.
choices.explanation: Forking stale context does not solve the need for a coherent current review.

choices.text: Use the old recommendation and add a disclaimer that some materials may have changed.
choices.explanation: The task is to produce a current recommendation, not qualify a stale one.

choices.text: Start a fresh session with a summary of the old review as background and require current source checks for changed materials.
choices.explanation: Correct. The old session is stale; a fresh run can use the prior summary without trusting outdated tool results.

skills: Resume named sessions for valid ongoing investigations.
Fork sessions for parallel strategy comparison.
Choose resume versus fresh summary based on staleness.
Tell resumed sessions exactly what changed.
knowledge: Named session resumption with --resume.
fork_session for independent branches from a shared baseline.
Resumed sessions need awareness of changed files or stale results.
Fresh sessions with summaries can be safer than stale resumption.
guideReferences: Domain 1, Task 1.7: Manage session state, resumption, and forking
questions.guideSummary: Fork sessions to compare divergent paths from a common baseline without polluting one path with another.
questions.prompt: A discovery session established requirements for a new workflow engine. The team now wants one branch to design a deterministic state machine and another to design an agent-led orchestration layer. Both need the shared requirements, but each should reason independently. What should you do?

choices.text: Continue in the same session and alternate between the two designs every few turns.
choices.explanation: Alternating in one session can cross-contaminate assumptions and conclusions.

choices.text: Start two unrelated sessions and manually retype only the top three requirements.
choices.explanation: That loses the richer baseline the discovery session already established.

choices.text: Fork the discovery session into two sessions, one for each architecture option.
choices.explanation: Correct. Forking supports independent exploration from a shared baseline.

choices.text: Ask one branch to critique the other branch before it has developed its own design.
choices.explanation: That undermines independent exploration and does not use session forking.
